articles.description: On 1 July, an Airbus A330 lifts off from Harare for London under the

articles.title: Zimbabwe's tobacco anchor in Britain holds at £129m
articles.description: Of every £100 of goods Zimbabwe sells to Britain, £96 is tobacco or beverages. While the wider UK–Zimbabwe trade relationship softened in the year to December 2025, Zimbabwean tobacco held its grip, and the IMF projects Zimbabwean GDP growth of 7.5% in 2025 and 5.0% in 2026. The product that built Zimbabwe's name as an exporter is still doing the work.

articles.title: The Apple of haircare gets its German cheque
articles.description: Ruka, the London-based textured-hair company founded by Zimbabwean entrepreneur Varaidzo Tendai Moyo and her co-founder Ugorji Agbai, has closed a $4.5m round co-led by Henkel Ventures and Freedom Trail Capital. The deal, which takes lifetime funding to roughly $10m and brings one of the world's largest consumer-goods groups onto the cap table, is the most meaningful endorsement yet of a Zimbabwean diaspora-founded brand.

more.title: Pardon Mapondera: gone but not forgotten at Venice
more.description: The fifth and final piece in our series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Pardon Mapondera, the artist whose practice the room finally arranges itself around, treats discarded plastic and glass jars not as ecological signal but as ritual matter, and produces, in the process, the most quietly devotional installation in the Biennale this year.

more.title: Gideon Gomo: from stone to bronze, the ancestral algorithm at Venice
more.description: The fourth in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Gideon Gomo's first sustained body of bronze work, paired with discarded vehicle number plates and Zimbabwean springstone, is the move of a sculptor at full maturity rethinking the material vocabulary of his country's most internationally legible art form.

more.title: Shumba Maasai in Venice
more.description: At the 61st Biennale, the Zimbabwean artist's day-long procession through the city's campi, performed under the borrowed authority of a Murehwa chief, restages the social-sculpture lineage of Joseph Beuys for a contemporary African discursive practice.

more.title: Franklyn Dzingai: family albums and the neural commons at Venice
more.description: The third in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Franklyn Dzingai's reductive cardboard prints, built from the family album, the magazine clipping, the back-room studio photographs of mid-century Zimbabwe, turn out, on close looking, to be the most precisely engineered work in the room.

more.title: Felix Shumba: charcoal and the practice of disappearance at Venice
more.description: The second in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Felix Shumba's six-metre charcoal wall and his Telephone Reader series anchor a body of work that argues drawing is not the act of capturing the seen, but the act of staying present to what is on its way out of sight.

more.title: UK services share in Zimbabwe rises to 8.2%
more.description: The headline UK services trade with Zimbabwe softened year on year. The structural number underneath it tells the better story: Britain's share of all services Zimbabwe imports rose 2.4 percentage points to 8.2%, the highest measured level in years. As Zimbabwean buyers re-rate their global supplier mix, the UK is taking a bigger slice of the country's services demand.

more.title: Zimbabwe's demand lifts British exports by 14.6%
more.description: The Zimbabwean economy is in its strongest stretch in years: 7.5% GDP growth in 2025, a projected 5% in 2026, and that growth is pulling demand for cars, commercial vehicles, medicines and industrial machinery in with it. UK goods exports to Zimbabwe rose 14.6% on the year, the strongest line in an otherwise soft bilateral picture for Britain.

more.title: Zimbabwe adds freight rail option to lithium export gateway
more.description: Zimbabwe's State-owned railway company said on Tuesday its collaboration with private operators now provides capacity to haul lithium concentrate to Maputo port in Mozambique by rail, expanding logistics options for the battery mineral. Africa's top lithium producer Zimbabwe mostly trucks the bulk mineral to port, a more expensive option fraught with logistical bottlenecks.

more.title: Victoria Falls Rainforest temporarily restricted due to active elephant
more.description: Rutendo Nyeve rutendo.nyeve@sundaynews.co.zw THE Zimbabwe Parks and Wildlife Management Authority (ZimParks) has announced a temporary restriction of access to the Victoria Falls Rainforest following reports of an elephant roaming in the area. The popular tourist site, a major attraction within the renowned UNESCO World Heritage Site, has been closed as a precautionary measure to ensure public safety. ZimParks spokesperson, Mr Luckmore Safuli, confirmed the development, saying the closure is essential to safeguard both visitors and staff. The authority’s teams are actively monitoring the situation to manage the risk of human-wildlife conflict. The restriction has been instituted as a precautionary measure to safeguard visitors, staff, and wildlife, and to reduce the risk of human-wildlife conflict, said Mr Safuli. ZimParks teams are closely monitoring the situation and will advise the public once it is safe to reopen access to the site. Members of the public and tour operators have been urged to comply with instructions from ZimParks personnel. Members of the public are urged not to enter the restricted area, approach the elephant, or interfere with ongoing wildlife management efforts, Mr Safuli said. ZimParks has reiterated its commitment to the conservation of Zimbabwe's wildlife heritage while ensuring the safety of the public. Further updates on the reopening of the rainforest are expected to be communicated through official ZimParks channels.

more.title: Government, IOM strengthen partnership on climate-induced migration
more.description: Sikhumbuzo Moyo smoyo@chronicle.co.zw THE Government and the International Organization for Migration (IOM) have reaffirmed their commitment to strengthening collaboration on addressing the growing impacts of climate change on human mobility, environmental degradation and sustainable development. The commitment was made during a high-level courtesy call between the Permanent Secretary in the Ministry of Environment, Climate and Wildlife, Mr Simon Masanga, and IOM Zimbabwe Head of Mission Ms Diana Cartier. The meeting focused on the increasing link between climate change, environmental degradation and migration, with both parties agreeing that climate-induced displacement and rural-to-urban migration are becoming pressing challenges requiring coordinated national and international responses. Discussions highlighted how environmental degradation is increasingly forcing people to leave their communities in search of better livelihoods, placing additional pressure on urban centres and natural resources. The two parties also explored opportunities for joint interventions with development partners, including UN-Habitat, to address the environmental and socio-economic effects of migration. The duo further discussed the need to tackle growing environmental challenges such as biodiversity loss, the impacts of artisanal mining and issues surrounding loss and damage associated with climate change. Recognising the importance of mobilising adequate resources, the Ministry and IOM agreed to strengthen strategic partnerships in the climate finance sector through comprehensive stakeholder mapping aimed at identifying key institutions and funding opportunities. Research and innovation also featured prominently during the discussions, with the two sides underscoring the need to scale up research and development to generate evidence that can support informed policy-making and programme implementation. The Ministry acknowledged IOM's ongoing research initiatives, saying the findings would contribute significantly to evidence-based decision-making and the effective implementation of climate-related programmes. Mr Masanga also expressed appreciation for IOM's technical contributions towards the development of the Climate Change Management Bill, noting that the organisation had played an important role in ensuring that migration and human mobility issues are incorporated into the proposed legislation. The meeting further agreed on the need to strengthen communication and public awareness on climate-induced migration through enhanced institutional collaboration, knowledge sharing and the use of specialised communication toolkits and shared media platforms. The engagement marked another step in the Government's efforts to integrate human mobility into national climate policy as Zimbabwe continues to strengthen resilience against the effects of climate change. Through enhanced collaboration, evidence-based research, progressive legislation and improved access to climate finance, the Ministry and IOM said they remain committed to protecting vulnerable communities and promoting sustainable environmental management in the face of increasing climate-related challenges.
